State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Administration     •     Executive/Legal     •     Enforcement     •     Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660               322-5901          322-6441               322-6444

April 26, 1983


Ms. Julie SoRelle
City Clerk
P.O. BOX 2440
Clearlake, CA  95422

Re: Our Advice No. A-83-106

Dear Ms. SoRelle:

Thank you for your request for advice on the conflict of
interests provisions of the Political Reform Act. The facts, as
presented to me, are that a recall election is going to be held
involving four Clearlake councilmembers. You are the City Clerk
and you want to make and participate in the decisions related to
this recall election. The City Attorney indicated that you
should disqualify yourself from any involvement because one of
the changes hoped to be achieved by the recall election is a
redefinition of the position of City Clerk, with a corresponding
increase in salary. You have asked whether your participation
in the recall decisions will involve a conflict of interest.

The Political Reform Act prevents a public official from
making, or participating in the making of, a governmental
decision in which he knows, or has reason to know, he has a
financial interest. (Government Code Section 3710.)1/ An
official has a "financial interest" in a decision within the
meaning of Section 3710 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will materially affect a source of income aggregating
$250 or more received by the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made.

Section 82030(b)(2) excludes from the definition of "income"
salary received from a local government agency. Thus, the

________________
1/ Hereinafter all statutory references made are to the
Government Code unless otherwise noted.

Ms. Julie SoRelle
April 26, 1983
Page 2


salary that you receive from the City of Clearlake is not
considered to be income to you and you are not prohibited from
making and participating in decisions related to the recall
election which may, ultimately, affect your salary.2/

I hope I have clarified this matter for you. If you have
any additional questions, please feel free to contact me at
916/322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel, Legal Division

2/ It is unnecessary for me to consider the issue of
"foreseeability."

My analysis is limited to the provisions of the Political
Reform Act and it does not include any consideration of the
Election Code sections that you cited in your letter.

CITY COUNCIL MEMBERS
John J. Lucich, Jr., Mayor
Eugene Achter, Vice Mayor
Dr Thomas M. Hewlett
Dore C. Logotela
Ed Robey, Jr

City of Clearlake
Post Office Box 2440
Clearlake, California 95422

(707) 994-8201

CITY CLERK
Julie SoRelle

April 6, 1983

Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Attn: Robert Leidigh

Re: City Clerk - City of Clearlake
    Conflict of Interest

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

Pursuant to our telephone conversation, I am requesting advice
on a conflict of interest issue under the provisions of
Government Code Section 83114(b).

I have enclosed the following for your review:

1. 3/18/83 memo from City Attorney to City Clerk (re-
   garding conflict of interest).
2. Newspaper articles concerning possible recall and
   interest in reorganization of City Government (not
   a complete file - further information may be ob-
   tained from the Clear Lake Observer).
3. Notice of Intent to Recall filed 3/8/83.
4. 3/21/83 letter of advice from Elections Division
   of Secretary of State's Office (regarding conduct
   of recall).
5. 2/28/83 memo from City Attorney (regarding conduct
   of recall).
6. 3/18/83 memo from City Attorney to City Council
   (regarding services of County Clerk).
7. My 3/30/83 letter to Clearlake Observer (I do not
   know if the letter has been or will be published.)

The original Notices of Intent filed 3/8/83 failed to contain

Fair Political Practices Commission
April 6, 1983
Page 2


the provisions of Elections Code Section 27023 and the pub-
lication was also incomplete. New Notices of Intent to Re-
call four councilmen were filed yesterday with my Deputy.
Two sets of Notices of Intent were filed on 4/5/83 - one
with the same proponents (for a "by district" recall) and
another set with entirely different individuals (for an "at
large" recall). All of the notices contained the exact word-
ing as the original notices for recall.

As you will see, there are conflicting opinions as to the
manner in which the recall proceedings should be conducted
at this time -- at large or by district. Mr. Thunen, in his
3/18/83 memo to the City Council, states that the County
Clerk is prepared to verify signatures on recall petitions.
This statement is not entirely accurate. The County Clerk
has advised me that she does not have the voter registrations
divided into our City Districts and would be unable at this
time to verify signatures if Districts 2 and 4 had to be re-
called in that manner.

At present the City Clerk of Clearlake receives $150.00 per
month salary as set by Resolution of The City Council. She
is to perform all duties outlined in the California Codes
with the exception of accounting duties which have been trans-
ferred to an appointed Director of Finance. In addition she
acts as secretary to the City Council.

I feel there is no conflict of interest in performing my
duties as City Clerk in accordance with Elections Code sec-
tions relating to recall proceedings.

Please advise if, in your opinion, there is sufficient reason
to declare a conflict of interest in this matter. I would
also appreciate a legal interpretation of my responsibility
for the actions of my Deputy City Clerk in the event I am
found in conflict of interest and she is requested to make the
necessary decisions on the recall proceedings.

Thank you for your cooperation and an early response.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Julie SoRelle
City Clerk

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

April 19, 1983

Julie SoRelle
Clearlake City Clerk
P.O. Box 2440
Clearlake, CA 95422

Re: A-83-106

Dear Ms. SoRelle:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McKean, an attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or unless more information is needed to answer your request, you should expect a response within 21 working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

CITY COUNCIL MEMBERS
John J. Lovich Jr. Mayor
Eugene Achor, Vice Mayor
Dr. Thomas M. Hewlett
Doris C. Logoteta
Ed Robey, Jr.

City of Clearlake
Post Office Box 2440
Clearlake, California 95422

(707) 994-8201

March 18, 1983

CITY CLERK
Julie SoRelle


TO:         JULIE SO RELLE

FROM:       CITY ATTORNEY

SUBJECT:    CONFLICT OF INTEREST; PENDING RECALL PROCEEDINGS

In view of the fact that considerable publicity has been given
to the position of the Clearlake Citizens' Committee that one
of the changes in municipal government which they hope to achieve by means of the pending recall is redefinition of the
job of the City Clerk and a corresponding increase in the salary for that position, I conclude that Government Code Section
87100 prohibits you from making any decision related to the
validity of the recall proceedings at any stage of the proceeding.

Government Code Section 87103 defines financial interest as
including any source of income of $250.00 or more which was
promised within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is to be made. The case of Witt vs. Morrow, 70 Cal. Ap.
3rd 817 (1977) indicates that it is not necessary to prove that
the decision of the official will have a significant impact on
the financial interest, but rather only that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect. It must be stated that it is now reasonably foreseeable that a successful recall election will have a material
effect on your compensation as City Clerk over the balance of
your four year term.

I am mindful of the fact that Government Code Section 87101
provides that Section 87100 does not apply where there is no
one else to make the decision. However, it is also true that
Elections Code Section 22003 permits the governing body of any
city to request the Board of Supervisors of the county to permit the County Clerk to render specified services to the city
relating to the conduct of an election. I have contacted the
County Clerk, who has stated that she is willing to perform
the task of verifying signatures on the various recall petitions. Further, pursuant to Ordinance No. 9, Section 3, and

JULIE SO RELLE
March 18, 1983
Page 2




Government Code Section 7, you may delegate any task re-
lated to this recall to your deputy, who does not appear
to have any financial interest in the outcome of the pend-
ing proceedings.

If you have any questions regarding this opinion, please
do not hesitate to contact me.


                                    RONALD V. THUNEN, JR.

Thursday, March 10, 1983
Price 25¢

Prosecution
opens against
Gerald Stanley

OROVILLE — Gerald Stanley bragged of being a professional hunter, but records of his long distance phone calls home were like animal migrations maps. Lake County investigators began probing the sniper murder of his wife Cindy.

Robert Crone, Lake County district attorney, said the observation while outlining the Aug. 1, 1981, murder in a day long opening statement Tuesday, Mar. 8. Crone told jurors he expects to prove that Stanley hid beneath a large cedar tree on a slight rise about 50 yards across Highway 20 from the swimming pool at Frank Spaig's resort in Nice.

There, Crone said, on that night when a shot rang out in the sullen summer stillness and Cynthia Rogers Stanley, then 29, who had become Stanley's fourth wife only 35 days earlier, slumped down in her lawn chair by the pool.

Friends, relatives and guests of the resort by the pool, her 11-year-old son Nathan was romping in the cabins, and one man, James Drager, in the pool, said he saw the muzzle flash under a tree across the road. Frank Spaig, Cindy's father, rushed inside for his M-1 rifle and fired twice in the air to make sure it worked as he ran toward the street, but the killer had gone.

William and Francis Shormacher, in a mobile on Burpee Road across Highway 20 from the pool, heard the shot right outside, Mrs. Shormacher

said they heard the shot and footsteps running and then a loud bump like a car going over the drainage ditch on the dirt landing that leads off Burpee Road. Crone said that's where Stanley's car was found.

Mrs. Shoemaker, still looking out the window, said she saw a car come down Burpee Road with its lights out. Her husband said it turned left on Highway 20 towards Lucerne.

The motive for the murder and why Crone seeks the death penalty for it is that Cindy was to testify as a victim against Stanley in court. She was hiding at the resort and it was one of the few moments in several days she had spent outside because of her fear of her husband. Crone said that sometimes moment by moment, second by second account of Stanley's whereabouts during that time period, Crone revealed long distance phone call records showed them California to his mother's home in Anderson and his mother's home in Hayford.

Sometimes there would be a one-minute call to the mother, followed a few minutes later by a longer call to a girlfriend in the state.

Crone said Stanley arranged for his mother to leave home because he was afraid her phone was tapped.

Recall initiated against
four council members

CLEARLAKE — An attempt to unseat City Councilmen Gene Achter, Mayor John Lucich Jr., Thomas Hayden and A. J. Olsen was initiated Tuesday with the filing of recall petitions with Deputy City Clerk Sharon Goode.

All four of the petition list the same reasons for recall — irresponsibility in allocating funds for public improvements and creating a hazard to public health and safety:

"He has shown a disregard for the city's financial soundness by approving expenditures for costly consultants and paying exorbitant salaries to city employees in positions of authority.

"He has established unnecessary staff positions that deplete the city's resources."

"He has shirked his elective responsibility by transferring unlimited power to appointed city managers and consultants who lack training and experience."

"He has proved unresponsive to ideas and concern expressed by the public in regard to ongoing city problems."

Mayor Lucich could not be reached for special attitude towards citizens who attend City Council meetings," the petition state.

The district will apply for federal disaster relief funds, Davidson said. From 70 to 90 percent of the storm damage costs could be required depending on the assessment made by the Federal Emergency Management Agency.

Although the Oaks's sewer system has been hit the hardest, the high water has concerned Gary Brown of Special Districts who is in charge of the other systems in the county. Brown noted that all his pump

Jerry Davidson of the Clearlake Oaks County Water District reports the district will apply for federal disaster relief funds.

RECALL

reached for comment because he was traveling to his place of employment as a Oakland city fireman.

Councilmember Doris Logoteta, the only council member not being recalled, would not comment on the matter.

Councilmen Ed Robey, Eugene Achter and Tom Hewlett could not be reached by phone for their reactions to the move that could remove them from the council.

A successful recall would require 25 percent of the total number of registered voters, the count to be based on the number reported at the last registration by the county clerk to the state.

After signed petitions are filed with the city clerk, the clerk must, within 30 days of the date filing, examine the signatures using a random sampling to include at least 500 of the percent of the signatures on the petition; they are then certified to the City Council.

Within 14 days after petitions are certified the council must issue an order stating that an election shall be held to determine whether or not the councilman named on the petition shall be recalled.

If the council fails to issue the order calling the election, the county clerk, within five days, shall set the date of the election which must be held not less than 88 nor more than 125 days after issuance of the order.

In the event of an insufficient petition, proponents have the right to examine it in order to determine which signatures were disqualified and why.

If a vacancy occurs in an office after a recall petition is filed against the vacating officer the recall election shall nevertheless proceed. The vacancy shall be filled as provided by law, the person to hold office only until a successor is elected.

Any voter who has signed a recall petition shall have his signature

withdrawn from the petition upon filing a written request with the clerk prior to the day the petition is filed.

Format of a city recall election ballot in addition to the name of the officer requires the question, "If the recall prevails shall the (name of legislative body, such as Clearlake City Council) fill the vacancy or vacancies by appointment or call a special election for that purpose?" Following the question the words "by appointment" and "by special election" shall appear on separate lines.

If less than a majority of the council (one or two members) is recalled the seats are immediately vacated and the remainder of the council would appoint the replacement if the majority of the voters favored that method or the remainder of the council would call an election to fill the vacancies.

The election must be called not less than 88 nor more than 103 days after the date of the order calling for the election. If a regular municipal election is to occur not more than 118 nor less than 88 days from the date of canvassing the vote (on the recall petition) the council may provide for filling the vacancy at the regular municipal election instead of at a special election. The next regular

municipal election in Clearlake will take place the first Tuesday in April, 1984.

The recalled officer may not be a candidate to succeed himself or herself at a special election held to fill the vacancy created by his or her recall may only be appointed by the legislative body to fill the vacancy.

If a majority of the council is recalled at the same election (three or more) the members recalled shall retain their offices until their successors are elected and qualified. Immediately after the canvass of votes the clerk shall call a special election to fill the vacancies arising as a result of the recall election to be held within 89 days after canvass of votes of the recall election has taken place.

In the event of filling vacancies created by recall by election nominations of candidates shall be made in the same way as in a regular election. Nomination papers and declaration of candidacy shall, in each case, be filed not less than 59 days prior to the date of the election and not before the day the order of the election is issued.

For a recall to be effective one half or more of the votes cast must favor the recall.

County is growing fast

Lake County was the eighth fastest growing county in California new figures released by the California Department of Finance show. Lake County's population is recorded at 41,100, Mendocino County 69,900. The report states that Lake County grew by five percent from July 1, 1981, to July 1, 1982. In comparison, Mendocino County ranked 33rd, growing by only 2.2 percent. The department reports that the state's population is an estimated 24,724,000, representing an increase of 2.1 percent per year.

County to apply for $600,000

Clear Lake Observer, Thursday, March 17, 1983—5

CCC chairman defends recall

CLEARLAKE — Teresa Rifesi, chairman pro-tem of the Clearlake Citizens Committee, told members of the Newcomer's Club Monday noon that a new city council could turn things around giving the city government that would produce more direct benefits for the people of the city rather than for a select group of employees.

Rifesi gave a brief review of the committee's organization stating that its purpose has been to serve the community by giving it information regarding what the people in the city of Clearlake wanted. "Things didn't turn quite that way," she commented.

The former Clearlake city clerk said the committee now numbers in the hundreds; however, total membership and names of members are not divulged, she said, in order to protect them from possible reprisals.

She defended the committee's recall effort against four city councilmen, Mayor Jack Lucich, Gene Asher, Dr. Hewlett and Ed Robey, on the grounds that while recall is not an attractive way to go it is the only recourse the people have.

"We (committee members) were told by the city attorney that the people had nothing to say about the way the city was being run. This was a shock, indeed. We were told we had nothing to say and that our only recourse was recall. We couldn't believe this. So the directors journeyed to Sacramento to find out where the people stood only to learn there that recall was the only path open to them.

"Recall doesn't deserve the reputation it has because it is the vehicle provided by law whereby the people can assert their will. It gives the citizens the opportunity to decide if they like what is going on or not."

Rifesi added that recall was something she had personally always abhorred, but under present circumstances she likened it to an opportunity to make changes if the majority of the people concur.

She brought out the present allocation of money by the council as one major cause for the recall. She maintained that a conservative council could redo the city budget making more money available for vitally needed services, particularly in the public works department. She was critical of the generous salaries being paid to department heads. As an example she cited the position of building inspector who has no staff under him who receives $25,320 per year plus a recently granted 3.2 percent increase. The similar position in Lakeport pays $18,000 and the individual oversees three other employees and has been there a number of years. The Clearlake building official received his training at city expense and was a former employee of PMI with less than two year's experience, she said.

Rifesi supports a city government with an elected city clerk in charge of the clerical functions of the city stating this would be less costly than the present set-up and the clerk would be directly responsible to the people as an elected official.

When asked about the cost of the recall, an estimated $7,000 for each of two elections if two are needed, she said in her view the new council could effect savings that would more than offset this cost.

"We are not seeing the benefits we expected from incorporation," Rifesi averted. She was a member of the Incorporation Committee. "I had seen when working for the county the small amount of money generated in the Clearlake community that was returned to this area and therefore I was a strong supporter of incorporation. Never in my wildest dreams could I have foreseen what we now have in the form of city government," she declared.

She assured the audience that the recall was not a personal attack on any of the men involved but that they were instrumental in delegating absolute authority to their appointed city manager and for allocating funds in a manner that brought too little direct benefit to the people.

Earlier in her talk she declared that the committee has never set it-

self up as representing all of the people of Clearlake. She said this would be a mistake and the committee has never taken such a position.

She then referred to the telephone surveys undertaken among city registered voters. "We contacted at least 20 percent of the registered voters and the results were alarming. Eighty percent of those called said they were dissatisfied with the present city government. She said that calls to more than 200 committee members also revealed a majority in favor of recall."

Rifesi, in conclusion, said "If you the people vote the recall down then that is wonderful, that is what the majority of you want." She said that no one on the committee's board of directors is aspiring for public office but noted that there were many qualified people in town who would make good candidates if the recall were successful.

She invited the group to attend, if they were interested, a citizens committee meeting at the Senior Nutrition Center, Lakeshore Dr., Friday, Mar. 18, at 7 p.m.

By Sanford Nax

CLEARLAKE — The
erection of a large sign near

another councilman said
the sign was a "cheap shot"
at city hall.

committee's president pro

believes the city council is
delegating too much power
to a high-priced city staff.

"We want to canvas the
people in the community to
find out what they
want ... We want to know

or a different concept,"

nford Nax
EARLAKE — Fighting
ity is hard work, but if
is committed to the
s the fight can be won,
Pat Bowler, a self-
ied "boat rocker" who
was visiting here
ay night.

t front of you," Bowler
he Clearlake Citizens

ler is a member of

Observer notifies city
of Brown Act violation

By Bonny Jean Hanchett

CLEARLAKE — After reviewing provisions of the Brown Act, California's open meeting law, the Clear Lake Observer Editor notified city hall Monday, Jan. 17, that the city council had been in violation of this statute by conducting interviews with the nine candidates for the planning commission vacancy in closed session.

When questioned about the matter Planning Director Jack Burrell said he was not aware of this provision. The Observer then asked City Attorney Don Thueen to advise the council. At the time, he was asked to call in discuss the matter. Later, when not hearing from him, Plain City Attorney Roy Thueen was contacted. He said that he had been made aware of the situation and would advise the council at the next meeting Monday night that a violation had occurred and that the entire procedure, one with interviews, to be conducted over again, following a duly noticed meeting open to the public.

The meeting in question was held on Saturday at city hall. In talking with some of the nine applicants we learned that the council had not been advised of the rule to interview aspirants for the planning commission post in public.

The Brown Act does not extend the provision allowing closed sessions to discussions of personnel matters. Those people who occupy these positions will wield considerable power on behalf of the public thus the public, in turn, has a right to know their attributes and deficiencies. Therefore, interviews and voting on the candidates for this position must be done in public after giving public notice. His vote also must be public and cannot be made by secret ballot, the law provides.

Following the appointment of Roy Burrell, a vacancy, created by the resignation of businessman, Gary Jensen, will be nullified and the council has been advised by Thueen to combine interviews for the Jensen vacancy with those for the three vacancies that will occur on the planning commission in March.

LAKE COUNTY — Valley Architects' rough estimate is that the cost of remodeling the interior of the county courthouse will cost an extra $21,000, not including engineering fees.

According to testimony received before the board Tuesday, the steel shelf for the new fourth floor addition of more steel to form cantilevers in order to provide needed floor space and to permit access to the new third and fourth floors from the existing structure was outlined by Paul Schnell, Valley Architects, based in St. Helena, told the board. "The cantilevering is required to get access from the main building to the new'' County officials were unprepared to state how much the changes will impact the overall cost of the courthouse project which was originally expected to cost taxpayers $600,000. But the architects' recommendations came in time to halt the concrete pouring which had been scheduled for Wednesday, according to the architect.

CLEARLAKE — When it was brought to light by the Clear Lake Observer early Monday that the Clearlake City Council had violated the Brown Act (California's open meetings law) by interviewing candidates for the planning commission vacancy in closed session Jan. 8, the confirmation of the council's choice, Roy Burrell, scheduled for Monday night, had to be cancelled.

The council set the Jan. 8 date at the regular meeting Jan. 3.

City Councilman Roy Burrell advised the City Counsel Don Thueen prior to that session of the Brown Act provisions, according to Councilman [illegible] Logeath.

Councilman Tom Hewlett remarked, during a recess Monday evening, "I should have known it would be against the Brown Act," he said.

The council had spent close to four hours interviewing nine aspirants for the vacancy, including Vera [illegible], Margaret Thomason, Del Felters, Deu Sandoval, Albert Reuss, Richard Pangborn and Joe Ross, in addition to Burrell.

Thueen also recommended to the council that applicants for three other planning commission seats due'' to vacancies be interviewed at the same time as the council interviews candidates for the present vacancy. The three seats are now held by Leo Park, Jim Kennedy and Jim Herrmann.

He also suggested that the council schedule interviews for the March vacancies as well as for the present vacancy created by the recent resignation of Gary Jensen. The city attorney also said incumbents should be asked to indicate in writing if they wish to be reappointed for a four-year term.

TO: City Clerk, City of Clearlake, Lake County, California

TO: Councilman Eugene Achter, City of Clearlake Councilmanic
     District #4, Lake County, California

NOTICE OF INTENTION TO PETITION FOR THE RECALL OF

Councilman Eugene Achter, Clearlake City Council

- - - - - - - - - - - - - - -

For the following reasons:

     1. He has shown irresponsibility in allocating the city's money
by voting to budget insufficient funds for public works, i. e. road and
drainage maintenance, thereby creating a threat to the public health and
safety.

     2. He has shown disregard for the city's fiscal well being by
approving expenditures for costly consultants fees and by approving for city
employees in managerial capacities exorbitant salaries that are above and
beyond the city's ability to pay and still meet the needs of the people.

     3. He has further depleted the city's available resources by
establishing unnecessary staff positions.

     4. He has shirked his responsibility as an elected representative
of the people of the City of Clearlake by transferring unlimited authority to
appointed city staff administrators who lack the training and experience needed
to assume such powers.

     5. He has proved unresponsive to ideas and concerns expressed by
the public in regard to ongoing city problems.

     6. He has often displayed an arrogant and disrespectful attitude
toward citizens who attend council meetings.

     We believe Councilman Eugene Achter should be recalled.

March 21, 1983

Ms. Julie SoRelle
City Clerk
City of Clearlake
Post Office Box 2440
Clearlake, California 95422

Dear Ms. SoRelle:

Your letter of February 25 raised the question of whether a city
council member may be recalled by voters of the member's district
or of the entire city, where the member had been elected by district,
but where at the time of that election the city's voters had
determined to select council members at large in future elections.

The question appears not to have been decided by statute or the
courts to date. After reviewing applicable statutes and case law
governing election of municipal officers and recall, we have
concluded that courts faced with the question would most likely
decide that such council members must be recalled by district.

Government Code Section 34870 and following sections authorize
voters of cities to determine to select council members by or from
districts, and to amend that determination. Under the statutory
scheme, municipal legislative bodies may submit an ordinance to the
city's voters providing for election of the body's members by or from
a specified number of districts (Government Code Section 34871) and
may amend or repeal the ordinance in the same manner (Section 34873).

The statutory restriction is spelled out: No amendatory ordinance
may be submitted to the voters if one or more entire legislative
districts are eliminated prior to the termination of the term of office
of the councilman of or from the district (Government Code Section
34875(b)). In support of this requirement, the Code states that
ordinances establishing districts may only be amended or repealed
if the term of office of any council members is not affected
(Government Code Section 34873).

The statutory requirement that changes in district boundaries not be
effected during any incumbent's term is mandatory. Local offices are

Ms. Julie SoRelle
Page 2
March 21, 1983


subject to regulation by the state legislature as authorized by the
constitution. Cal. Const. Art. XI Sec. 2; See People v. Branvard
(1865) 27 Cal. 470; In Re Bulger (1873) 45 Cal. 553. Therefore,
local officers have no power to act in conflict with general law
without legislative authority to do so being delegated to them.
See Proulx v. Graves (1904) 143 Cal. 243, 246.

The City of Clearlake has authority to, and did, enact an ordinance
repealing districts. But such ordinance may not become effective
as to any incumbent until the expiration of the incumbent's term.
Therefore, the ordinance's language that city council members be
elected at large "in future elections" can only be interpreted to
refer to future elections occurring at the end of the incumbent's
term.

Since the incumbent's district boundaries are not repealed until the
expiration of the term, any recall proceeding commenced during the
term must necessarily be carried out by voters living within the
boundaries of the district from which the incumbent was elected.
The electoral jurisdiction of the officer sought to be recalled
still lies within the "old" boundaries during the incumbent's term
for recall purposes. See Elections Code Section 27004.

This conclusion based on the district selection statute is consistent
with the law governing filling vacancies arising by resignation,
death or otherwise. If a vacancy should arise in a seat for reasons
other than recall, it would be filled based on the boundaries exist­
ing at the beginning of the term. Sloan v. Donoghue (1942) 20 Cal.
2d 607. If the vacancy were to be filled by election (Government
Code Section 36512, 36512.1), voters would be qualified to vote in
the vacancy election who resided within the boundaries existing at
the beginning of the term.

The apparent public policy protects the right of voters, who have
selected a representative, to retain an elected official accountable
to them during the term for which the incumbent was elected. The
conclusion above regarding general law city recall is consistent with
this policy.

The only exception to the policy is that the body which created the
office may abolish or change it at any time (People v. Branvard, supra,
27 Cal. 470) and incumbents take office subject to that restriction.
Martello v. Superior Court (1927) 202 Cal. 400, 408. The exception
does not apply in the instant case because the authority authorizing
the creation of the city and its governing board, the Legislature, has
not amended the boundaries. While the Legislature has delegated power

Ms. Julie SoRelle
Page 3
March 21, 1983


to the city and its voters (Government Code Section 34870 - 34884) to
amend boundaries, as it has authority to do (See Proulx v. Graves,
supra, 143 Cal. 243, 246) the delegated authority and any concomitant
restrictions must be observed.

The conclusion regarding proceedings being based on "old" district
boundaries also avoids a logical inconsistency encountered if a
different conclusion is adopted which gives effect to the boundary
changes immediately. The ordinance here in question approved at-large
selection "in future elections". City councils have authority to
fill recall-caused vacancies by appointment if voters authorize that
method. Elections Code Section 27311. Vacancies arising for other
reasons may be filled by appointment. Government Code Section 36512.
By its terms, the ordinance adopted by Clearlake's voters in 1982
would not apply to such appointments. No logical reason appears to
justify different boundary lines being used to select a successor
when the vacancy is filled by election rather than by appointment.
Indeed, recall election voters would undoubtedly be confused upon
being instructed that their vote to fill recall vacancies by election
meant that a new incumbent would be accountable to a different set of
voters than if they chose to fill the vacancy by appointment.

The only language potentially suggesting that the change to an at-large
constituency may not be effected immediately is found in Government
Code Section 34878:

"If a majority of the registered voters of the city, who vote,
vote in favor of the ordinance, at the expiration of the terms
of office of the members of the legislative body, or as
provided by ordinance, members of the legislative body shall
be elected by (or from) the districts described, or by or
from districts with an elective mayor, and in the manner
provided." (Emphasis added).

The emphasized language is ambiguous because it is not clear to what
"ordinance" it refers.

However, the reference to "ordinance" is undoubtedly not a reference
to the very ordinance repealing the districts. To interpret it

Ms. Julie SoRelle
Page 4
March 21, 1983


otherwise would render useless the prohibitions in Government Code
Section 34873 and 34875 against affecting current terms of office,
a conclusion to be avoided especially since the latter two Code
sections apply specifically to amendatory ordinances repealing
district lines, and the former ambiguous section refers to ordinances
adopting district boundaries.

Very truly yours,

DEBORAH SEILER
Assistant to the Secretary of State
Elections and Political Reform

[signature]

RICHARD B. MANESS
Staff Counsel

TO:        Julie SoRelle, City Clerk

FROM:      Ronald V. Thunen, Jr., City Attorney

RE:        Possible Recall of Councilman Achter

_________________________________________________________________


     Local newspapers have reported a possible attempt to
recall Councilman Achter. You have asked whether Councilman
Achter would be recalled by district or at large.

     Facts: When Clearlake was incorporated in November,
1980, five councilmembers were elected at large. At that
election, voters were also asked to vote on two
propositions: B. Shall members of the city Council in
future elections be elected by districts? C. Shall members
of the City Council in future elections be elected at large?

     We are informed that proposition B passed, but that the
vote on proposition C indicates that some voters probably
voted "yes" to both measures: Accordingly, it was
determined that at the 1982 municipal general election, two
councilmen would be elected by district. (Gov. Code Section
34884. Councilman Achter, as one of the two members elected
with the lowest vote, was one of the two original
councilmembers whose term was scheduled to expire.

     The Council, by resolution No. 81-54 established
councilmanic districts prior to the time for filing
nomination papers for the 1982 municipal general election.
At the same time, the Council, believing that the question
of district versus at large election of councilmembers had
been presented to the voters in a confusing manner in 1980
(No pre-election publicity had been given to the issue, as
attention had focused primarily on the question of
incorporation itself, and secondarily on the council
candidates themselves, to the exclusion of other issues.)
passed resolution No. 81-53, placing the following
proposition on the same ballot:

     "Shall members of the City Council be elected at large
in future elections?"

     Councilman Achter filed for election from District No.
4, ran unopposed, and was elected. The ballot proposition

sections.facts:
When Clearlake was incorporated in November, 
1980, five councilmembers were elected at large. 
At that 
election, voters were also asked to vote on two 
propositions: B. 
Shall members of the city Council in 
future elections be elected by districts? 
C. 
Shall members 
of the City Council in future elections be elected at large? 
We are informed that proposition B passed, but that the 
vote on proposition C indicates that some voters probably 
voted "yes" to both measures: 
Accordingly, it was 
determined that at the 1982 municipal general election, two 
councilmen would be elected by district. 
Gov. Code Section 
34884. 
Councilman Achter, as one of the two members elected 
with the lowest vote, was one of the two original 
councilmembers whose term was scheduled to expire. 
The Council, by resolution No. 81-54 established 
councilmanic districts prior to the time for filing 
nomination papers for the 1982 municipal general election. 
At the same time, the Council, believing that the question 
of district versus at large election of councilmembers had 
been presented to the voters in a confusing manner in 1980 
(No pre-election publicity had been given to the issue, as 
attention had focused primarily on the question of 
incorporation itself, and secondarily on the council 
candidates themselves, to the exclusion of other issues.) 
passed resolution No. 81-53, placing the following 
proposition on the same ballot: 
"Shall members of the City Council be elected at large 
in future elections?" 
Councilman Achter filed for election from District No. 
4, ran unopposed, and was elected. 
The ballot proposition 


also passed by a 5 to 1 margin and in District 4, the 
proposition passed by a margin of 3 to 1, after receiving 
considerable pre-election publicity and discussion within 
the community. 
The issue thus presented by the current possible recall 
move is whether because Mr. Achter was elected by the voters 
of District 4, recall proceedings shall be restricted to 
registered voters in that district, or, because the voters 
of the whole city have expressed a preference for at large 
voting in all future elections, a recall should be conducted 
on an "at large" basis. 
I conclude that the will of the 
people as expressed by their vote in April 1982 should 
prevail, and that an "at large" recall would be the proper 
format. 
My analysis follows.
sections.analysis:
Normally, a councilman elected by district can only be 
recalled by district. 
El. C. Sec. 27211, Sec. 27004. 
In 
other words, the proponents, the signers of the petition, 
and voters at the recall election must 
all be registered 
voters in the councilmanic district. 
Moreover, if the 
decision is to recall and to re-fill the seat by a 
subsequent special election, that election would also be 
limited to the district. 
In this case, however, the voters have already rejected 
the district concept and strongly stated a preference for at 
large election. 
It would appear to frustrate the will of 
the people of the City to conduct any council election on a 
district basis after the 1982 election. 
Further, an anomaly 
currently exists in Clearlake, in that three councilmembers 
were elected at large, and two by districts, thereby giving 
the residents of districts two and four a disproportionate 
representation. * 
To hold Mr. Achter accountable at this 
time only to the voters of District 4 would be to perpetuate 
the second class status of the voters in former districts 1, 
3 and 5, as they will never have a representative of their 
own. 
Once the voters have elected to fill all future 
council vacancies by at large voting, the only fair approach 
to recall is to make all councilmembers accountable to the 
electorate at large immediately, rather than to wait for 
expiration of the term for which a councilman was elected by 
district. 
Any contrary result does violence to the 
cherished Constitutional concept of "one man, one vote" 
expressed in Baker v. Carr. 
In summary, I conclude that two well-established 
principles of American Constitutional law: (A) That the will 
of the people, as expressed at the polls, is to be 
effectuated insofar as is practicable; and (B) That the 
citizens of a community are entitled to equal voting power 
whenever practicable, dictate the conclusion that an "at 


large" recall format should be followed with respect to Mr. 
Achter's seat at this time. 
I am informed that you have received a tentative 
response from the Secretary of State's office indicating a 
contrary conclusion. 
I will submit this opinion to that 
office for their consideration. 
I do not believe that, if 
that office considers all of the matters discussed herein, 
they will disagree with my conclusion. 
cc: City Council 
Secretary of State 
RVT 
* There are approximately 5,000 registered voters in the 
City; lacking any truly valid census data as to the total 
number of persons in the city or each district (the 1980 
Census was conducted without regard to the boundaries or 
future needs of the City, as it did not exist then) the City 
Council attempted to divide the City into five districts by 
correlating the only available statistical data -
numbers 
of housing units and numbers of registered voters. 
Essentially, then, a voter in former districts 1, 3, or 5 
can be said to possess 3/25,000 of the voting power in the 
City, but a resident in District 4 now possesses 28/25,000 
of the voting power. 
A district recall continues this 
disproportion until 1986; an "at large" recall, even if 
unsuccessful at any stage, would effectively convert Mr. 
Achter's present district constituency into a citywide 
constituency, by making him answerable to all of the people, 
not just those who elected him, thereby cancelling the 
disporportional advantage now possessed by residents of 
former District 4.

CITY ATTORNEY 
SUBJECT: 
SERVICES OF COUNTY CLERK IN CONNECTION WITH 
PENDING RECALL PROCEEDINGS 
Elections Code Section 22003 provides as follows: 
"The governing body of any city ... may by resolu-
tion request the Board of Supervisors of the county 
to ?ermit the County Clerk to render specified ser-
vices to the city ... relating to the conduct of an 
election .... " 
I have contacted the County Clerk with regard to the pending 
recall proceedings to inquire whether or not she would be 
willing or able to provide any services to the city. 
She in-
forms me that because there are special district elections 
scheduled for this November, she does not believe that her 
staff can assist the city in the actual conduct of a recall 
election. 
However/ because the verification of sjgnatures 
nn a recall petition would take place early enough in the 
year so as not to conflict with the November elections, she 
is prepared to perform that service for the city provided 
that the necessary resolution is adopted by the Council. 
I enclose a copy of the County Clerk's estimate of the cost 
to perform this work. 
I also enclose a copy of a memoran-
dum I have given to our City Clerk indicating my belief that 
she would have a conflict of interest in the making of any 
decisions regarding the validity of any particular step in 
the recall proceedings. 
I recowmend tl1at the Council adopt the enclosed resolution. 
RONALD V. THUNEN, JR. 
RVT:vwp 


CiTY COUNCIL MEMBERS 
JOhn j 
lUCiCh, Jr Mayor 
Eugene AchIer, Vice Mayor 
Dr Tnomas M Hewletl 
00"5 C logote'a 
Eo Robey, Jr 
CITY CLERK 
JulJe SoRelle 
TO: 
FROM: 
SUBJECT: 
City of Clearlake 
Post Office Box 2440 
Clear,ake, California 95422 
(707) 994-8201 
March 18 1 
1983 
JULIE SO RELLE 
CITY ATTORNEY 
CONFLICT OF INTEREST; PENDING RECALL PROCEEDINGS 
In view of the fact that considerable publicity has been given 
to the position of the Clearlake Citizens' Committee that one 
of the changes in municipal government which they hope to a-
chieve by means of the pending recall is redefinition of the 
job of the City Clerk and a corresponding increase in the sal-
ary for that position, I conclude that Government Code Section 
87100 
prohibits you from making any decision relating to the 
validity of the recall proceedings at any stage of the pro-
ceeding. 
Government Code Section 87103 defines financial interest as 
including any source of income of $250.00 or more which was 
promised within twelve months prior to the time when the de-
cision is to be made. 
The case of Witt vs. Morrow, 70 Cal. Ap. 
3rd 817 (1977) indicates that it is not necessary to prove that 
the decision of the official will have a significant impact on 
the financial interest, but rather only that it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect. 
It must be stated that it is now reasonably foresee-
able that a successful recall election will have a material 
effect on your compensation as City Clerk over the balance of 
your four year term. 
,I am mindful of the fact that Government Code Section 87101 
provides that Section 87100 does not apply where there is no 
one else to make the decision. 
However, it is also true that 
Elections Code Section 22003 permits the governing body of any 
city to request the Board of Supervisors of the county to per-
mit the County Clerk to render specified services to the city 
relating to the conduct of an election. 
I have contacted the 
County Clerk, who has stated that she is willing to perform 
the task of verifying signatures on the various recall peti-
tions. 
Further l 
pursuant to Ordinance No. 9 1 Section 3, and 


JULIE SO RELLE 
March 18, 1983  
Government Code Section 7, you may delegate any task re-
lated to this recall to your deputy, who does not appear 
to have any financial interest in the outcome of the pend-
ing proceedings.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City Clerk can participate in recall election decisions when the recall campaign seeks to redefine the City Clerk position and increase its salary, which could materially affect the Clerk's financial interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Government Code Section 82030(b)(2) excludes salary from local government agencies from the definition of 'income,' so the City Clerk may participate in recall election decisions despite potential effects on her salary.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; LLM: Document contains both incoming request (April 6, 1983 letter from City Clerk) and FPPC response (April 26, 1983 advice letter). City Attorney memo (March 18, 1983) recommends disqualification based on different legal analysis. Newspaper clipping included but not relevant to FPPC analysis. Extraction focuses on FPPC advice letter as primary document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You are the City Clerk and you want to make and participate in the decisions related to this recall election. The City Attorney indicated that you should disqualify yourself from any involvement because one of the changes hoped to be achieved by the recall election is a redefinition of the position of City Clerk, with a corresponding increase in salary. You have asked whether your participation in the recall decisions will involve a conflict of interest.

CONCLUSION: above regarding general law city recall is consistent with 
this policy. 
The only exception to the policy is that the body which created the 
office may abolish or change it at any time (People v. Branvard, supra, 
27 Cal. 470) and incumbents take office subject to that restriction. 
Martello v. Superior Court (1927) 202 Cal. 400,408. 
The exception 
does not apply in the instant case because the authority authorizing 
the creation of the city and its governing board, the Legislature, has 
not amended the boundaries. 
While the Legislature has delegated power 


Ms. Julie SoRelle  
March 21, 1983 
to the city and its voters (Government Code Section 34870 - 34884) to 
amend boundaries, as it has authority to do (See Proulx v. Graves, 
supra, 143 Cal. 243, 246) the delegated authority and any concomitant 
restrictions must be observed. 
The conclusion regarding proceedings being based on "old" district 
boundaries also avoids a logical inconsistency encountered if a 
different conclusion is adopted which gives effect to the boundary 
changes immediately. 
The ordinance here in question approved at-large 
selection "in future elections". 
City councils have authority to 
fill recall-caused vacancies by appointment if voters authorize that 
method. 
Elections Code Section 27311. 
Vacancies arising for other 
reasons may be filled by appointment. 
Government Code Section 36512. 
By its terms, the ordinance adopted by Clearlake's voters in 1982 
would not apply to such appointments. 
No logical reason appears to 
justify different boundary lines being used to select a successor 
when the vacancy is filled by election rather than by appointment. 
Indeed, recall election voters would undoubtedly be confused upon 
being instructed that their vote to fill recall vacancies by election 
meant that a new incumbent would be accountable to a different set of 
voters than if they chose to fill the vacancy by appointment. 
The only language potentially suggesting that the change to an at-large 
constituency may not be effected immediately is found in Government 
Code Section 34878: 
"If a majority of the registered voters of the city, who vote, 
vote in favor of the ordinance, at the expiration of the terms 
of office of the members of the legislative body, or as 
rovided b ordinance, members of the legislative body shall 
be elected by or from} the districts described, or by or 
from districts with an elective mayor, and in the manner 
provided. II 
(Emphasis added). 
The emphasized language is ambiguous because it is not clear to what 
"ord i nance" it refers. 
However, the reference to "ordinance" is undoubtedly not a reference 
to the very ordinance repealing the districts. 
To interpret it 


Ms. Julie SoRelle  
March 21, 1983 
otherwise would render useless the prohibitions in Government Code 
Section 34873 and 34875 against affecting current terms of office, 
a conclusion to be avoided especially since the latter two Code 
sections apply specifically to amendatory ordinances repealing 
district lines, and the former ambiguous section refers to ordinances 
adopting district boundaries.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Administration • Executive/Legal • Enforcement • Statement of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 April 26, 1983 Ms. Julie SoRelle City Clerk P.O. BOX 2440 Clearlake, CA 95422 Re: Our Advice No. A-83-106 Dear Ms. SoRelle: Thank you for your request for advice on the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act. The facts, as presented to me, are that a recall election is going to be held involving four Clearlake councilmembers. You are the City Clerk and you want to make and participate in the decisions related to this recall election. The City Attorney indicated that you should disqualify yourself from any involvement because one of the changes hoped to be achieved by the recall election is a redefinition of the position of City Clerk, with a corresponding increase in salary. You have asked whether your participation in the recall decisions will involve a conflict of interest. The Political Reform Act prevents a public official from making, or participating in the making of, a governmental decision in which he knows, or has reason to know, he has a financial interest. (Government Code Section 3710.)1/ An official has a "financial interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 3710 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will materially affect a source of income aggregating $250 or more received by the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. Section 82030(b)(2) excludes from the definition of "income" salary received from a local government agency. Thus, the ________________ 1/ Hereinafter all statutory references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. Ms. Julie SoRelle April 26, 1983 Page 2 salary that you receive from the City of Clearlake is not considered to be income to you and you are not prohibited from making and participating in decisions related to the recall election which may, ultimately, affect your salary.2/ I hope I have clarified this matter for you. If you have any additional questions, please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5901. Very truly yours, Janis Shank McLean Janis Shank McLean Counsel, Legal Division JSM:km 2/ It is unnecessary for me to consider the issue of "foreseeability." My analysis is limited to the provisions of the Political Reform Act and it does not include any consideration of the Election Code sections that you cited in your letter. CITY COUNCIL MEMBERS John J. Lucich, Jr., Mayor Eugene Achter, Vice Mayor Dr Thomas M. Hewlett Dore C. Logotela Ed Robey, Jr City of Clearlake Post Office Box 2440 Clearlake, California 95422 (707) 994-8201 CITY CLERK Julie SoRelle April 6, 1983 Fair Political Practices Commission P. O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 Attn: Robert Leidigh Re: City Clerk - City of Clearlake Conflict of Interest Dear Mr. Leidigh: Pursuant to our telephone conversation, I am requesting advice on a conflict of
embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-83-106 addresses whether City Clerk Julie SoRelle has a conflict of interest participating in recall election decisions when the recall campaign seeks to redefine her position and increase her salary. The Commission concluded no conflict exists because local government salary is excluded from the statutory definition of 'income.'
